Name: OptimizedTensorCoreSparsity
Title: Optimizing Sparse Matrix Multiplication for Tensor Core GPUs
Short Hypothesis: Sparse matrix multiplication (SpMM) can be significantly accelerated on Tensor Core GPUs by optimizing the use of shared memory, register allocation, and bandwidth utilization. By leveraging a novel sparse format and kernel design, we can reduce the overhead of indexing and improve the performance of SpMM for various sparsity levels.
Related Work: Recent work in SpMM optimization for GPUs, such as SpInfer and At-Scale Sparse Deep Neural Network Inference, has shown promising results in reducing memory overhead and improving computational efficiency. However, these methods often do not fully exploit the capabilities of Tensor Core GPUs, particularly in terms of shared memory and register allocation. Our proposal aims to bridge this gap by introducing a new sparse format and kernel design specifically optimized for Tensor Core architectures.
Abstract: Sparse matrix multiplication (SpMM) is a critical operation in large language model (LLM) inference, but existing methods often suffer from high indexing overhead and suboptimal performance on Tensor Core GPUs. We propose Optimized Tensor Core Sparsity (OTCS), a novel framework that introduces a tensor-core-aware sparse format and optimized kernel design to reduce indexing overhead and improve computational efficiency. OTCS leverages shared memory, register allocation, and bandwidth utilization to achieve significant performance gains across a range of sparsity levels. Our experiments show that OTCS outperforms state-of-the-art SpMM implementations on NVIDIA A100 GPUs, achieving up to 2.14x speedup over Flash-LLM and up to 2.27x speedup over SparTA. We also demonstrate the practical benefits of OTCS in end-to-end LLM inference, achieving up to 1.58x improvement in throughput and latency.
Experiments: 1. **Sparse Format Design**: Develop a new sparse format that minimizes indexing overhead and is optimized for Tensor Core architectures. This format will be compared against existing sparse formats such as CSR, CSC, and COO in terms of memory usage and computational efficiency.

2. **Kernel Optimization**: Design and implement a SpMM kernel that leverages shared memory, register allocation, and bandwidth utilization to achieve optimal performance. The kernel will be profiled using nvprof and Nsight to identify and optimize key performance bottlenecks.

3. **Performance Evaluation**: Evaluate the performance of the OTCS framework on NVIDIA A100 GPUs across various sparsity levels (30% to 70%). Measure and report p50/p99 latency, throughput under batching, and energy per token. Compare the results against state-of-the-art SpMM implementations such as Flash-LLM and SparTA.

4. **End-to-End Inference**: Integrate the optimized SpMM kernel into a full LLM inference pipeline and evaluate its impact on end-to-end performance. Measure and report the improvements in throughput, latency, and energy efficiency for popular LLMs such as BERT and T5.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. **Hardware Dependency**: The proposed framework is highly dependent on the specific architecture of Tensor Core GPUs. It may not generalize well to other GPU architectures or non-Tensor Core GPUs.

2. **Sparsity Level Sensitivity**: The performance gains may vary significantly across different sparsity levels. We need to ensure that the framework is robust and performs well even at lower sparsity levels.

3. **Overhead of New Sparse Format**: The new sparse format may introduce additional overhead in terms of data transformation and memory management. We need to carefully evaluate and optimize this overhead to ensure that it does not negate the performance gains.
